coredata.dc:title: Dramatically enhanced solar-driven water splitting of BiVO4 photoanode via strengthening hole transfer and light harvesting by co-modification of CQDs and ultrathin β-FeOOH layers
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Tingsheng
Chen, Shuai
Wang, Jiachen
Zhang, Yan
Li, Jinhua
Bai, Jing
Zhou, Baoxue
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen generation by solar-driven water splitting is considered as a promising strategy to address energy crisisandenvironmental emission issues. Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is a highly promising photoanode material for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting, but its severe bulk and surface charge recombination, sluggish oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics and narrow visible light harvesting are still bottlenecks. Here, an excellent CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode was designed by co-modification of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and ultrathin β-FeOOH layers (<10 nm) on BiVO4 to tackle the above issues. The CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 shows dramatically enhanced photocurrent, which is 10.7 and 2.98 times higher than BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4 at 0.8 V vs. RHE (VRHE), with negatively shifted onset potential of 448 and 255 mV, respectively. The maximum incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is 6.7 and 1.86 times higher than that of BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4, respectively. Additionally, the surface hole injection efficiency (η surface) of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is 7.1 and 2.1 times higher than that of BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4 at 0.8 VRHE, respectively. The results can be attributed to three effects: (i) Synergetic catalysis of CQDs and FeOOH sharply improves the OER kinetics due to the introduction of high-density oxygen vacancies (Ov); (ii) The CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction efficiently suppresses the bulk charge recombination; (iii) CQDs significantly boost the light harvesting both in the ultraviolet and visible regions.

head.ce:title: Dramatically enhanced solar-driven water splitting of BiVO4 photoanode via strengthening hole transfer and light harvesting by co-modification of CQDs and ultrathin β-FeOOH layers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen generation by solar-driven water splitting is considered as a promising strategy to address energy crisisandenvironmental emission issues. Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is a highly promising photoanode material for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting, but its severe bulk and surface charge recombination, sluggish oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics and narrow visible light harvesting are still bottlenecks. Here, an excellent CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode was designed by co-modification of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and ultrathin β-FeOOH layers (<10 nm) on BiVO4 to tackle the above issues. The CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 shows dramatically enhanced photocurrent, which is 10.7 and 2.98 times higher than BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4 at 0.8 V vs. RHE (VRHE), with negatively shifted onset potential of 448 and 255 mV, respectively. The maximum incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is 6.7 and 1.86 times higher than that of BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4, respectively. Additionally, the surface hole injection efficiency (η surface) of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is 7.1 and 2.1 times higher than that of BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4 at 0.8 VRHE, respectively. The results can be attributed to three effects: (i) Synergetic catalysis of CQDs and FeOOH sharply improves the OER kinetics due to the introduction of high-density oxygen vacancies (Ov); (ii) The CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction efficiently suppresses the bulk charge recombination; (iii) CQDs significantly boost the light harvesting both in the ultraviolet and visible regions.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen generation by solar-driven water splitting can effectively achievethegoal of storing solar energy inchemical bonds, which is considered as a promising strategy to address energy crisisandenvironmental emission issues [1–8]. Solar-to-hydrogen (STH) efficiency of a photoelectrochemical (PEC) device is primarily determined by the properties of photoanode materials for the fact that the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is more kinetically demanding than the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in the process of PEC water splitting [5,9–11]. Among the alternative photoanodes, the monoclinic bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) has attracted a great deal of interest due to its moderate band gap energy (E g, 2.4–2.5 eV), favorable band-edge positions, low-cost and modest stability in neutral electrolytes [12–17]. However, the reported photocurrent densities of pure BiVO4 photoanodes are much lower than their theoretical maximum, which is limited by the severe bulk and surface charge recombination, and the sluggish OER kinetics due to the low electron and hole mobility (~1 × 10-2 and ~ 4 × 10-2 cm2 V−1 s−1, respectively), short electron and hole diffusion length (~10 and ~ 100 nm, respectively) [12,13,15,16,18–20].

To address the issues, massive efforts have been made to boost the PEC performances of BiVO4 photoanodes, including doping [21], introduction of oxygen vacancies (Ov) [15,22], morphology or crystal facet engineering [12,23,24], construction of heterojunction [25] and surface modification of oxygen evolution catalysts (OECs) [8,11,14,19,26–32], etc. In general, surface modification of OECs is an important strategy to promote the PEC activities of BiVO4 photoanodes by effectively passivating the surface charge recombination centers and enhancing the interfacial OER kinetic for PEC water oxidation [11,19,20,33,34]. Among them, iron oxyhydroxide (FeOOH) has been intensively studied as a promising OEC material due to its high activity, low cost, natural abundance and environmental friendliness [35,36]. It has been found that the crystalline phase of FeOOH (α-, β-, and δ-) plays a crucial role in improving its electrocatalytic OER performance [35,37]. Recently, J. Hu et al. [35] successfully prepared different crystalline phases through a controlled solvothermal route, finding that the β-FeOOH provides the best electrocatalytic OER activity due to higher density of Ov.

On the other hand, it has been reported that the Ov in OECs could greatly upgrade the electrocatalytic OER activity due to increased surface active sites and higher surface energy [19,38]. When the Ov–rich OEC is coated on a photoanode, an outward driving force is induced by Ov for trapping holes and driving hole transfer, lowering the activation energy of OER on the photoanode/electrolyte interface. For example, B. Zhang et al. [19] reported the BiVO4 photoanode modified with ultrathin and Ov-rich β-FeOOH, which showed increased photocurrent density of 4.25 mA cm−2 at 1.23 vs. RHE (VRHE) for PEC water oxidation in comparison to the amorphous-FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode (2.25 mA cm−2). Besides, the Ov on OEC surface can offer connected channels for intermediate oxygen species, which are electrochemically active for OER [39]. Therefore, increasing the Ov density on OEC surface may be a promising strategy to further improve the OER activity.

Recently, it was found that the OEC activity for electrocatalytic OER is remarkably improved after incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which is ascribed to the increased Ov density originated from CQDs [39]. For instance, G. Li et al. [39], D. Tang et al. [40], S. Zhao et al. [41] and L. Tian et al. [42] prepared the OECs of NiFe layered double-hydroxide, SnO2/Co3O4 composite, MnO2 nanoflower and Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ perovskite nanofibers, respectively, all finding that their electrocatalytic OER activities were remarkably boosted after incorporation of CQDs. Meantime, CQDs can form heterojunctions with the semiconductors with appropriateband-edge positions, which can efficiently suppress the bulk charge recombination, such as the recently reported CQDs/ZnO [1,43], CQDs/TiO2 [44,45], CQDs/WO3 [46], CQDs/Fe2O3 [47], CQDs/g-C3N4 [48] and CQDs/BiVO4 [49,50], etc. Moreover, CQDs can efficiently broaden the visible light harvesting range of the photoanodes [1,43,44,46,48–50].

Inspired by the above discussion, an excellent CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode was designed by co-modification of CQDs and ultrathin β-FeOOH layers (<10 nm) on BiVO4 (Scheme 1 ). After modification of CQDs on FeOOH/BiVO4, the increased Ov density (from 31.2% to 48.5%) results in sharply improved OER kinetics. Meanwhile, the CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction is formed due to their matched energy band alignment, which efficiently suppresses the bulk charge recombination. Additionally, CQDs significantly improve the light harvesting both in the ultraviolet and visible regions. Therefore, the CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is expected to demonstrate improved PEC performance. The photocurrent of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is 10.7 and 2.98 times higher than that of BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4 at 0.8 VRHE, with negatively shifted onset potential of 448 and 255 mV, respectively.

The CQDs were first characterized by transmission electronic microscope (TEM), Raman spectrum, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy (Fig. S1). It can be observed from the TEM image that the CQDs are uniformly distributed in the liquidphase with the average size of 1.75 nm (Fig. S1a-b). The lattice spacings of 0.234 and 0.235 nm for CQDs confirmed by the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) correspond to the (100) facet ofgraphite (Fig. S1c) [44,51]. Two sharp peaks centered at 1354 cm−1 and 1582 cm−1 are indexed to the D and G bands of carbon materials (Fig. S1d) [52–54]. The CQDs demonstrate an intrinsic light absorption edge of 606.2 nm, giving the E g of 2.05 eV (Fig. S1e). The CQDs show the PL spectra with the excitation wavelengths of 350, 400, 450, 500 550 and 600 nm, respectively (Fig. S1f). The peaks of PL emission spectra shift to higher wavelengths with increased excitation wavelengths, which is the characteristic of defective surface emission originated from CQDs [44,45,50]. The crystal structures of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD). The diffraction peaks appearing at 26.59°, 33.87°, 37.72°, 51.83°, 61.72° and 65.89° are well indexed to SnO2 originating from the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) substrates (JCPDS #46–1088) (Fig. S2). Except for the SnO2 peaks, all other diffraction peaks of the films can be well indexed to the monoclinic BiVO4 (JCPDS #14–0688). In addition, no obvious diffractions of FeOOH and CQDs were detected for FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4, possibly resulting from the small amount of decorated FeOOH or/and CQDs.

To confirm the successful modification of CQDs and FeOOH on BiVO4, the HRTEM and the scanning electron microscope (SEM) with the energy dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS) were performed (Fig. 1 ). As shown in Fig. 1a, the HRTEM image of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 shows well-formed monocrystal with the interplanar spacings of 0.23, 0.26 and 0.47 nm, corresponding to the (211), (200) and (011) planes of the monoclinic BiVO4, respectively [19,55,56]. The lattice spacing of 0.32 nm corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [39,54,56]. In addition, the FeOOH layers with the thickness of 2–7 nm are clearly observed, which compactly attach on BiVO4 surface. Furthermore, the lattice spacing of 0.16 nm corresponds to the (521) plane of β-FeOOH [19]. Fig. 1b shows the cross-sectional SEM image of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 with the thickness of 255 nm. The red dotted box is the selectedarea forsurfacescanning, inside which is the overlap of EDS elemental mapping. The corresponding EDS elemental mapping results confirm the existence of Bi, V, Fe, O, C and Sn elements with a uniform distribution on the surface of FTO substrate, which indicates a relatively homogenous coverage of FeOOH and CQDs on the surface of BiVO4 with the relative Fe and C content of 3.56 and 8.30 wt%, respectively (Fig. 1c-h, Fig. S3 and Table S1). For comparison, the EDS, EDS elemental mapping and relative elements content of the top-view SEM images for pure BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 are exhibited in Fig. S4-S7 and compared in Tables S2–S3. The Bi, V, O and Sn elements are detected in pure BiVO4, while Bi, V, O, Fe, C and Sn elements are detected in CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4, once again suggesting the successful decoration of FeOOH and CQDs on the surface of BiVO4. It is worth mentioning that the Sn element is originated from the FTO substrates, which is detected for the thin thicknesses (top-view SEM images of BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4) and the covered area of FTO substrate (cross-sectional SEM image of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4).

To gain insights into the mechanism of the synergistic effects of CQDs and FeOOH, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to investigate the interaction and charge transfer in the photoanodes. The XPS scan survey spectra of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 are displayed in Fig. S8. The typical O 1 s, V 2p, and Bi 4f peaks are characteristics of BiVO4.The emerging Fe 2p peaks in FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 indicate the successful incorporation of FeOOH on the surface of BiVO4. The relatively weaker C 1 s peaks of BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4 are ascribed to the carbonaceous contamination. The improved intensity of C 1 s peak for CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is found after CQDs modification due to the additional carbon from CQDs. High resolution XPS spectra of Bi 4f, V 2p and O 1 s for BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 are shown in Fig. 2 a, Fig. 2b and Fig. 2c, respectively. For the pristine BiVO4, the Bi 4f shows the binding energies (BE) of 158.8 eV for Bi 4f7/2 and 164.1 eV for Bi 4f5/2, while the V 2p shows the BE of 516.5 eV for V 2p3/2 and 524.2 eV for V 2p1/2, which are the characteristics of Bi3+ and V5+ in BiVO4, respectively [25,33,57]. The BE of the Bi 4f and V 2p do not shift for FeOOH/BiVO4, suggesting no change observed in the valence state of Bi and V after modification of FeOOH.

However, the BE of Bi 4f and V 2p in CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 both shift to lower values (158.4 eV for Bi 4f7/2, 163.7 eV for Bi 4f5/2, 516.2 eV for V 2p3/2 and 523.8 eV for V 2p1/2, respectively). The result is possibly ascribed to the increased electronegativity of Bi and V atoms that the electrons transfer from the electron-rich CQDs toBi and V atoms [43], which is evidenced by the fact that the BE of C 1 s for CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 shift to a higher value in comparison to that for BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4 (Fig. S8). For the O 1 s spectrum in BiVO4, only one peak centered at 529.5 eV is observed, which is attributed to the O2− species in the lattice (OL) [19,22]. For the O 1 s spectra in FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4, two peaks can be clearly identified, in which the lower BE at 529.5 eV is attributed to the O2− species in the OL and the higher BE at 531.6 eV is assigned to the Ov [19,22]. The oxygen species in BiVO4 is 100% of OL. With growth of Ov-rich β-FeOOH on BiVO4, the Ov is 31.2% of the total oxygen species. With further modification of CQDs on β-FeOOH/BiVO4, the the Ov is increased to 48.5%, in which the additional Ov is originated from the CQDs [39]. The CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 shows the Fe 2p peaks (711.2 eV for Fe 2p3/2 and 725.6 eV for Fe 2p1/2, respectively) and Fe 3p peak (56.3 eV) that are consistent with FeOOH (Fig. 2d and Fig. 2e). As shown in Fig. 2f, three peaks located at 284.8, 286.1, and 288.1 eV are assigned to the C − C, C − O, and O = C-O groups, respectively, suggesting the existence of CQDs [1,44].

To get the optimal load of FeOOH or CQDs on BiVO4, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of BiVO4 modified with different load of FeOOH or CQDs are first compared in Fig. S9 and Fig. S10, respectively. Clearly, the FeOOH/BiVO4 prepared with 10 mM of FeCl3·6H2O (FeOOH-10/BiVO4) and the CQDs/BiVO4 prepared with 2 times of spin coating of CQDs (CQDs-2/BiVO4) show the highest photocurrent densities, respectively. For convenience, the FeOOH-10/BiVO4 and CQDs-2/BiVO4 are denoted as FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4, respectively, in the whole paper. The LSV curves of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 measured in 0.2 M Na2SO4 under illumination of 100 mW cm−2 are shown in Fig. 3 a. It clearly reveals that the BiVO4 modified with FeOOH (FeOOH/BiVO4) or CQDs (CQDs/BiVO4) shows greatly increased photocurrent densities with significant negatively shifted onset potentials. With further modification of CQDs on FeOOH/BiVO4, the resulting CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 shows much steeper and larger photocurrent density. The onset potentials and photocurrent densities at different potentials for the photoanodes are compared in Fig. 3b and Fig. S11. The onset potentials of FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 are almost the same, which are negatively shifted by about 190 mV compared with the pristine BiVO4 (0.978 VRHE). The onset potential of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is negatively shifted by 448 mV, suggesting that co-modification of CQDs and FeOOH greatly reduces the onset potential. The CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 shows the photocurrent densities of 1.40 mA cm−2 at 0.8 VRHE, 2.53 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE and 3.39 mA cm−2 at 1.6 VRHE, which are 10.7, 4.16 and 2.73 times higher than BiVO4, 2.98, 1.58 and 1.39 times higher than FeOOH/BiVO4, and 3.01, 1.91 and 1.51 times higher than CQDs/BiVO4, respectively. Additionally, the bare CQDs only shows a low photocurrent density of 0.16 mA cm−2 (Fig. S12). Besides, the FeOOH/CQDs/BiVO4 was also perpared and its PEC performance was compared with CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 as shown in Fig. S13. The photocurrent of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is higher than that of FeOOH/CQDs/BiVO4, which is possibly due to the much higher OER activity by modification of CQDs on the surface of FeOOH OEC. To further highlight the improvement of the BiVO4 photoanode by co-modification of CQDs and FeOOH, some recent advances in BiVO4-based photoanodes for PEC water splitting are compared with the CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode in our work. Because there is a big difference among the PEC activities of the pristine BiVO4 photoanodes in the reported literatures depending on the synthesis methods, procedures, etc., the improvement of the modified BiVO4 photoanodes is mainly evaluated by the increased times of photocurrent (ITP) in comparison to the pristine BiVO4 photoanodes at 0.8 and 1.23 VRHE, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3c and summarized in Table S4, the photocurrent of the CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode in our work is 10.7 and 4.16 times higher than of the pristine BiVO4 photoanode at 0.8 and 1.23 VRHE, respectively, which are higher than the values in most of the reported works, such as WCoFe/BiVO4 [8], Rh-SrTiO3/BiVO4 [11], NiOOH/FeOOH/BiVO4 [14], β-FeOOH/BiVO4 [19], Fe-Pi/BiVO4 [26], NiOOH/BiVO4 [27], FeCoOx/BiVO4 [28], MnCo2O4/BiVO4 [29], CoF2-F/BiVO4 [30], BiVO4–Fe/CoP [31], CoNi-MOFs/BiVO4 [32], CQDs/BiVO4 [49], NiOOH/FeOOH/CQDs/BiVO4 [50] and NiOOH/FeOOH/CQDs/BQDs/BiVO4 [58], suggesting the excellent improvement of the BiVO4 photoanode by co-modification of CQDs and FeOOH in our work.

The transient photocurrent responses of the photoanodes were studied by chronoamperometriccurrent method at 1.23 VRHE under illumination with chopping (Fig. 3d). The results are in good agreement with the LSV data as shown in Fig. 3a. In addition, a sharp transient spike was observed for the pristine BiVO4 upon removing the lightbarrier, because the photogenerated holes accumulate at the surface and then recombine with the photogenerated electrons [44,59]. However, the transient spikes are obviously attenuated for FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4, indicating that surface modification of FeOOH or/and CQDs can efficiently suppress the recombination of the holes reaching the surface of BiVO4 photoanode.

Assuming 100% of Faradaic efficiency,the applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) can be calculated by the equation [59]:
(1)

where J is the photocurrent density (mA cm−2), V is the applied bias (VRHE) and P is the incident light power density (100 mW cm−2). As shown in Fig. 3e, the maximum ABPE of BiVO4 is only 0.06% at 1.01 VRHE, while the ABPE of FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 can reach the maximum of 0.23% at 0.93 VRHE and 0.21% at 0.87 VRHE, respectively. In contrast, the maximum ABPE of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is further increased to 0.60% at a lower potential of 0.79 VRHE, which is 10, 2.6 and 2.9 times higher than that of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4, respectively. The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) was tested as a function of the incident light wavelength (300–800 nm). The IPCE can be calculated by the formula [21]:
(2)

where J(λ), λ and P(λ) are the measured photocurrent density at specific wavelength, the wavelength of incident light and the measured light power density at that wavelength, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3f, the maximum IPCE of BiVO4 is 6.36% at the wavelength of 460 nm. With decoration of FeOOH or CQDs, the maximum IPCE of the resulting FeOOH/BiVO4 or CQDs/BiVO4 is significantly increased to 22.9% at 400 nm or 19.9% at 370 nm, respectively. Noticeably, the photocurrent response of CQDs/BiVO4 is increased in the visible region, which is attributed to the increased visible light harvesting by CQDs. The CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 demonstrates significantly improved photocurrent response both in the ultraviolet and visible regions, in which the maximum IPCE is further increased to 42.6%, which is 6.7, 1.86 and 2.14 times higher than that of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4, respectively.

The light absorption properties of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 were measured over the wavelength range of 300–800 nm by the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 4 a). The UV–vis absorption spectra of FeOOH/BiVO4 and BiVO4 nearly coincide with the absorption edge of 502 nm, which is possibly ascribed to the ultrathin thickness of FeOOH layers. After decoration of CQDs, the resulting CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 show obvious red shift and significantly increased light harvesting both in in the ultraviolet and visible regions, with the absorption edge of 512 nm. The IPCE of CQDs/BiVO4 is more than twice of BiVO4, but the absorbance is relatively little increased. This is because the greatly enhanced IPCE of CQDs/BiVO4 is simultaneously affected by three factors from CQDs: (i) enhanced OER kinetics; (ii) increased suppression of the bulk charge recombination and (iii) improved light harvesting. Therefore, the IPCE of CQDs/BiVO4 does not increase linearly withthe increased absorbance. The E g can be calculated by the following formula [29]:
(3)
α h v = A
hv -
E g
n / 2
where α, h, v, A and E g are the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, light frequency,proportionality constant and band gap energy, respectively. n is the characteristic integer, depending on the characteristics of the optical transition in a semiconductor. Because BiVO4 is a direct semiconductor, the value of n is 1 [29]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the E g values of BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4 are estimated to be 2.47 eV. After modification of CQDs, the E g values of CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 are decreased to 2.42 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carriers separation and transfer kinetics processes
Bulk separation and surface injection efficiencies
ce:section.ce:para:
To reveal the reasons of significantly improved photocurrent of BiVO4 photoanode after co-modification of CQDs and FeOOH, the bulk charge separation and surface charge transfer behaviors were investigated. The photocurrent density for water oxidation (J PEC) can be expressed as [12,14,44,59]:
(4)
J
PEC
=
J
abs
×
η
bulk
×
η
surface
where J abs is the photocurrent density calculated by assuming 100% conversion of the absorbed photons to current density; the bulk charge separation efficiency (η bulk) is the yield of the photogenerated holes reaching the surface and the surface charge injection efficiency (η surface) is the yield of the surface holes injected into theelectrolyte. The J abs can be estimated from AM 1.5G solar spectrum (Fig. S14a) and light harvesting efficiency (LHE, Fig. S14b) according to the following equations [44]:
(5)
J
abs
=
∫
300

λ 1240

(6)

where λ is the wavelength (nm); λe is the absorption edge wavelength of the photoanodes; N ph (λ) is the photon flux (mW m−2 nm−1); A (λ) is the absorbance at wavelength λ. The LSV curves of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 for sulfite oxidation under illumination of 100 mW cm−2 are shown in Fig. 5 a. According to the UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 4a, the λe of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 are 502, 502, 512 and 512 nm, giving the J abs of 4.98, 4.93, 5.81 and 5.75 mA cm−2, respectively (Fig. 5b). In the presence of hole scavenger (Na2SO3), the surface recombination of charge carriers can be completely suppressed without influencing the η bulk (η surface = 100%). Therefore, the η bulk and η surface can be calculated by the equations [12,44,59]:
(7)

where J H2O and J Na2SO3 are the photocurrent densities achieved by the oxidation of H2O and Na2SO3, respectively.

According to the equations above, the calculated η bulk and η surface are presented in Fig. 5c and Fig. 5d, respectively. After modification of CQDs on BiVO4, the η bulk of the resulting CQDs/BiVO4 (25.9% at 0.5 VRHE and 53.7% at 1.23 VRHE, respectively) has a obvious increase in comparison to that of the pristine BiVO4 (16.3% at 0.5 VRHE and 46.1% at 1.23 VRHE, respectively) due to the formation of CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction that promotes the bulk separation of photogenerated carriers. Compared with BiVO4 or CQDs/BiVO4, the η bulk of FeOOH/BiVO4 or CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 shows negligible change. Compared with the η surface of BiVO4 (8.93% at 0.8 VRHE and 26.7% at 1.23 VRHE), a remarkable improvement of the η surface is observed for FeOOH/BiVO4 (30.7% at 0.8 VRHE and 71.2% at 1.23 VRHE), CQDs/BiVO4 (20.5% at 0.8 VRHE and 42.4% at 1.23 VRHE) and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 (63.3% at 0.8 VRHE and 81.1% at 1.23 VRHE), suggesting the effect of CQDs as an OEC [44]. In particular, the η surface is dramatically increased after co-modification of CQDs and FeOOH, which is 7.1 and 3.0 times higher than that of the pristine BiVO4 at 0.8 and 1.23 VRHE, respectively. This is because the FeOOH reduces the OER overpotential and enhances the surface charge transfer kinetics of BiVO4, which are greatly strengthened by further modification of CQDs. With the synergistic effect of CQDs and FeOOH, the CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 shows dramatically enhanced PEC performance.

To gain more insight into the charge transfer kinetics at the electrode/electrolyte interface, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded at 1.23 VRHE under illumination (100 mW cm−2).The EIS Nyquist plots of the photoanodes are exhibited in Fig. 6 , with decreased radius in the order: BiVO4 > CQDs/BiVO4 > FeOOH/BiVO4 > CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4. The smallest radius of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 implies its faster charge transfer kinetics at the electrode/electrolyte interface during water oxidation [15,44]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 6, the EIS plots are fitted to an equivalent circuit model using Zview software, in which the Rs and Rct represent the series resistance and charge transfer resistance, respectively [18,44]. The CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 has the smallest Rct value of 188 Ω, compared with BiVO4 (764 Ω), FeOOH/BiVO4 (329 Ω) and CQDs/BiVO4 (465 Ω), demonstrating its significantly decreased interfacial charge transfer resistance. The EIS results suggest the significantly increased interfacial charge transfer kinetics of BiVO4 photoanode by co-modification of CQDs and FeOOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface charge transfer and recombination kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: The intensity modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) technique was further applied for probing the surface kinetics of the photoanodes, trying to disclose the origin of the significantly enhanced PEC performance of BiVO4 photoanode by co-modification of CQDs and FeOOH. IMPS is a powerful tool to study the surface kinetics of the photoelectrodes, which has been proved applicable for multi-step charge transfer processes [60]. J. Zhang et al. [61] successfully studied the Fe2O3 photoanode using IMPS technique with a simple model, in which the charge transfer only occurs at the surface while competing with the surface recombination. As shown in Fig. S15a, a typical IMPS Nyquist plot for an n-type photoanode consists of two semicircles located at the first and the fourth quadrants, in which two intercepts are observed at the first quadrant. The high frequency intercept (HFI) corresponds to the holes reaching the surface before they have a chance to recombine (reflected as hole current or transient photocurrent), and the low frequency intercept (LFI) represents the holes that are successfully injected into electrolyte for water oxidation (reflected as steady state photocurrent) [61]. When the frequency is decreased to the point of HFI and further, the surface charge transfer and recombination processes dominate (namely the semicircle in the first quadrant).

The IMPS Nyquist plots of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 recorded at different potentials are shown in Fig. 7 a and Fig. S15b-d. As the potential rises, all photoanodes show increased HFI and LFI, suggesting more holes arrive at the surface and more holes are efficiently injected into electrolyte for water oxidation at higher potentials. Furthermore, the IMPS Nyquist plots of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 at 1.2 VRHE are shown in Fig. 7b. The HFI values of BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4 are almost the same, which implies that the effect of FeOOH on the surface hole flux is ignorable. The same phenomenon is observed for CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 but with much higher HFI values due to the formation of CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction that efficiently suppresses the bulk recombination of photoinduced carriers. Compared with BiVO4, the FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 have higher LHI values, indicating that more holes participate in water oxidation after modification of FeOOH or CQDs. Compared with FeOOH/BiVO4, the CQDs/BiVO4 shows much higher HFI value but nearly identical LFI, suggesting its much lower holes utilization for water oxidation. However, compared with FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4, the LFI value of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is significantly increased, indicating much more injected holes for water oxidation after co-modification of CQDs and FeOOH. The above results areingoodaccordancewiththephotocurrent for water oxidation (Fig. 3a) and sulfite oxidation (Fig. 5a).

The ratio of LFI and HFI represents the charge transfer (injection) efficiency (η trans), which can be calculated by the equation [61,62]:
(9)
η
trans
= L F I / H F I =
k
trans
/
k
trans
+
k
rec
where k trans and k rec are the two surface rate constants for charge transfer and recombination, respectively. The η trans values of the photoanodes at different potentials are shown in Fig. 7c. The trend of the η trans values agrees well with the results in Fig. 5d. At 0.9 VRHE, the η trans of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is 77.7%, which is 6.37, 1.96 and 2.71 times higher than that of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4, respectively. At 1.2 VRHE, the η trans of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is 90.5%, which is 3.58, 1.27 and 1.81 times higher than that of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4, respectively. The frequency at the maximum imaginary part corresponds to the sum of k trans and k rec, which follows the equation [61,62]:
(10)
k
trans
+
k
rec
=
ω max
The k trans and k rec at different applied potentials can be obtained by Eqs. (9) and (10), which are shown in Fig. 7d and Fig. 7e, respectively. Over the potentials range from 0.9 to 1.3 VRHE, the k trans of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is 5.24–9.38, 1.41–2.00 and 2.50–3.84 times higher than that of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4, respectively. Meanwhile, the k rec of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is 2.63–6.64, 1.90–2.79 and 1.90–4.62 times lower than that of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4, respectively. The results suggest the best surface OER kinetic for CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4. TheCQDs/BiVO4 shows similar k trans behavior to FeOOH/BiVO4, further evidencing the effect of CQDs as an OEC [33]. The greatly decreased k rec for FeOOH/BiVO4 is ascribed to the passivation of FeOOH layers on the surface of BiVO4 that reduces the surface states and thus efficiently suppresses the recombination of surface holes [63]. In addition, theCQDs/BiVO4 demonstrates obvious decreased k rec, which possibly benefits from the formation of CQDs/BiVO4 CQDs heterojunction that results in the band bending at the surface of BiVO4, avoiding the internal band-to-band recombination and contributing to fast holes extraction toward the CQDs [33]. Therefore, the synergy of co-modification can enhance the η trans to a great extent. To verify which one (k trans or k rec) dominates the surface kinetics after co-modification, the increased rate (IR) of k trans and the decreased rate (DR) of k rec (vs. BiVO4) are compared by calculating their ratio values (IR of k trans/DR of k rec), which are exhibited in Fig. 7f. With increased potentials from 0.9 to 1.3 VRHE, the ratio values of FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 are decreased but higher than 1, suggesting that CQDs or FeOOH dominantly improves the k trans and that the domination of the increased k trans is decreased with higher potentials. The same trend is observed for CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4, but the ratio values fallbelow 1 when the potential is higher than 1.26 VRHE, indicating that the decreased k rec begins to dominate the surface kinetics. Asawhole, the ratio values follow the order: FeOOH/BiVO4 > CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 > CQDs/BiVO4, suggesting the highest effect of FeOOH on increasing the k trans instead of decreasing the k rec.

The electrocatalytic OER activities of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 were evaluated by LSV tests in 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution without illumination (Fig. 8 ). The LSV curves of the catalysts for OER are shown in Fig. 8a. Generally, the OER activity of a catalyst is assessed by the overpotential at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 [39]. As shown in Fig. 8b, the CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 displays the overpotential of 1.28 VRHE at the current density of 10 mA cm −2, which is lower than BiVO4 (1.89 VRHE), FeOOH/BiVO4 (1.63 VRHE) and CQDs/BiVO4 (1.75 VRHE), respectively. The current density of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is 9.72 mA cm−2 at 2.5 VRHE, which is much higher than that of BiVO4 (1.47 mA cm−2), FeOOH/BiVO4 (2.69 mA cm−2) and CQDs/BiVO4 (2.40 mA cm−2), respectively. As shown in Fig. 8c, the Tafel slope of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is 276 mV dec-1, which is smaller than that of BiVO4 (648 mV dec-1), FeOOH/BiVO4 (456 mV dec-1) and CQDs/BiVO4 (542 mV dec-1). The same trend is observed for the electrochemical resistance (Fig. 8d). The lowest Tafel slope and resistance suggest the fastest OER kinetics of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4. As shown in Fig. 2c, with growth of Ov-rich β-FeOOH on BiVO4, the Ov is increased from 0 to 31.2% of the total oxygen species. With further modification of CQDs on β-FeOOH/BiVO4, the the Ov is further increased to 48.5%. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 5d, the OER kinetics reflected by the η surface is a positive correlation with the Ov ratio of the photoanodes. Additionally, the the OER kinetics is further revealed by IMPS (Fig. 7) and electrocatalytic OER measurements (Fig. 8), which show the same trend with the results shown in Fig. 5d. Therefore, combined with the experimental results and the related literatures, it seems to offer evidence that the improved OER kinetic of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is ascribed to the increased Ov.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC stability and water splitting performance
ce:section.ce:para:
As seen in Fig. S16, the photocurrent of the pristine BiVO4 shows notable decay after 2 h of irradiation. In contrast, a high photocurrent density of 2.53 mA cm−2 for CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 at 1.23 VRHE is observed without showing any sign of decay after 2 h of continuous irradiation, proving its excellent stability (Fig. 9 a). The PEC water splitting performance of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 was tested via a quartz device with reactor and gas collection system (Fig. S17). The evolution amounts of H2 and O2 with a stoichiometric ratio of 2:1 are increased linearly with the illumination time. After 2 h of test, the total amounts of H2 and O2 are 90.4 and 44.2 μmol, respectively (Fig. 9b), which are close to the theoretical value. The Faradaic efficiency (ξ) can be calculated by the formula [59]:
(11)
ξ =
α F n
∫
t 1
t 2
J d t
× 100 %
where α is 2 for H2 and 4 for O2; F is the Faraday constant (96485C mol−1); n is the amount of H2 or O2 (mol); J is the photocurrent (A); t1 and t2 are the start and end time (s), respectively. The ξ of H2 and O2 evolution are about 96% and 94%, respectively, indicating the high conversion of photogenerated holes and electrons for water oxidation and reduction.

It is worth mentioning that the CQDs were not oxidized both for electrochemical and photoelectrochemical tests over the potential range from 0.2 to 2.0 VRHE due to the reasons below. (i) As shown on Fig. 9a, a high photocurrent density of 2.53 mA cm−2 for CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 at 1.23 VRHE is observed without showing any sign of decay after 2 h of continuous operation, proving that the CQDs were not oxidized. (ii) The Faradaic efficiency (ξ) of O2 evolution is about 94%, indicating that the photogenerated holes are mainly used for water oxidation instead of self-oxidation of the photoanode (Fig. 9b). (iii) At the potential below 1.7 VRHE for electrochemical OER measurements (Fig. 8a), no any gas for CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is generated, and no any oxidized peak is observed over the wide potentian range (0.2–1.7 VRHE). (iv) The successive LSV measurements of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 over the potential range from 0.2 to 2.0 VRHE under illumination or dark matches basically, suggesting that the CQDs were not oxidized over the entire potential range (0.2–2.0 VRHE) both for electrochemical and photoelectrochemical tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The proposed mechanism of co-modification-induced PEC activity
ce:section.ce:para:
To understand the charge transfer mechanism of CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction, it is necessary to determine the energy band alignment. According to the Mott-Schottky plots, the flat band potential (V FB) of BiVO4 can be calculated by the equation [21,59]:
(12)
1
C
2
=
2
e ε
ε
0
N
D
V -
V
FB
-
K
B
T
e
where C is the space charge capacitance; e is the fundamental electron charge; ε is the relative permittivity of the semiconductor; ε0 is the permittivity of vacuum; N D is the carrier density; V is the applied potential; K B is the Boltzmann constant and T is the absolute temperature. Obtained by the X-axis intercept, the V FB of BiVO4 is 0.10 VRHE (Fig. S18). Assuming that the gap between the bottom edge of the CB and V FB of the n-type semiconductor can be ignored, the CB potential of BiVO4 is estimated to be 0.1 VRHE, and the valance band (VB) is 2.57 VRHE [64]. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) was applied to estimate the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) level of CQDs, using the bare FTO and CQDs solution as the work electrode and electrolyte, respectively [44,65]. As shown in Fig. S19, the LUMO level of CQDs is − 0.37 eVRHE. According to the E g of 2.05 eV (Fig. S1e), the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) level of CQDs is 1.68 eVRHE. Therefore, a typical type-II energy band alignment for CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction is confirmed.

Based on the above discussion, the mechanism of the enhanced PEC performance for BiVO4 photoanode by co-modification of CQDs and FeOOH is proposed in Fig. 10 . As an OEC, the FeOOH significantly increases the OER kinetics of BiVO4. After further modification of CQDs on FeOOH/BiVO4, the type-II CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction is formed, in which the photogenerated electrons in the LUMO level of CQDs transfer to the CB of BiVO4 and further arrive at the Pt electrode via external circuit for hydrogen generation, while the photogenerated holes in the VB of BiVO4 transfer to the HOMO level of CQDs for oxygen evolution at the surface of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4, resulting in efficient separation of electron-hole pairs for solar-driven water splitting. Meanwhile, the OER kinetics is further improved due to the introduction of high-density Ov originated from CQDs. In addition, the visible light harvesting of BiVO4 can be improved by CQDs due to the upconversion effect. With the excitation of low-energy photons, the electrons in the π orbital jump into the high-energy π* orbital and then drop back into the lower energy state (σ orbital) because of the radiative relaxation, leading to the upconversion luminescence [44,66].

In summary, we have fabricated an excellent CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode by co-modification of CQDs and ultrathin β-FeOOH layers (<10 nm) on BiVO4. The PEC performance of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 is dramatically enhanced in comparison to that of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4, which is ascribed to three effects: (i) Synergetic catalysis of CQDs and FeOOH sharply improves the OER kinetics due to the introduction of high-density Ov; (ii) The CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction efficiently suppresses the bulk charge recombination; (iii) CQDs significantly boost the light harvesting both in the ultraviolet and visible regions. The two surface rate constants for k trans and k rec were investigated by IMPS, disclosing that co-modification of CQDs and FeOOH dominantly improves the k trans during the OER processes. Understanding the mechanism of co-modification-induced PEC activity may pave an avenue for the design and synthesis of highly efficient photoanodes for solar-driven water splitting.
